127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). But for
96	\emph{line bundles}, which are flat, that compatibility is \emph{elementary
97	flat-exactness}, not internal-hom machinery. This is exactly the carrier on
98	which the relative Picard group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
99	rests, and it is the subject of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,%
100	sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
101	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
102	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
103	
104	\section{Mathlib API survey}
105	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
106	
107	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
108	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
109	
110	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
111	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
112	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
113	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
114	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
115	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
116	sectionwise tensor product
117	\[
118	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
119	\]
120	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
121	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
122	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
123	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
124	
125	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
126	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
127	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
128	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
129	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
130	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
131	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
132	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
133	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
134	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
135	tensor-product operation
136	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
137	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
138	
139	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
140	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
141	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
142	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
143	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
144	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
145	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
146	\emph{flat-scoped restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
147	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
148	for line bundles \(L, M\) and the restriction along an open immersion
149	\(f\), the canonical comparison
150	\((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes M|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
151	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
152	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume, and for line bundles it is
153	elementary flat-exactness already available in Mathlib (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
154	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
155	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
156	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
157	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
158	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
159	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
160	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
161	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
162	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
163	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
164	independently of any upstream timing.
165	
166	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
167	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
168	
169	\begin{definition}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
173	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
175	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
176	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
177	  \[
178	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
179	  \]
180	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
181	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
182	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
183	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
184	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
185	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
186	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
187	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
188	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
189	  canonical compatibility
190	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
191	     \;\cong\;
192	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
193	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
194	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
195	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
196	
197	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
198	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
199	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
200	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
201	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
202	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
203	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
204	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
205	\end{definition}
206	
207	\begin{lemma}
208	
209	\leanok
210	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
211	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
213	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
214	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
215	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
216	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
217	  \[
218	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
219	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
220	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
221	  \]
222	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
223	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
224	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
225	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
226	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
227	  isomorphisms
228	  \[
229	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
230	    \qquad
231	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
232	  \]
233	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
234	  \[
235	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
236	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
237	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
238	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
239	  \]
240	  and a canonical braiding
241	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
242	  inherited from the corresponding data on
243	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
244	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
245	\end{lemma}
246	
247	\begin{proof}
248	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
249	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
250	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
251	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
252	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
253	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
254	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
255	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
256	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
257	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
258	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
259	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
260	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
261	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
262	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
263	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	\begin{remark}
267	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
268	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
269	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
270	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
271	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
272	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
273	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
274	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
275	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
276	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
277	  depend on it.
278	
279	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
280	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
281	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
282	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
283	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
284	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
285	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
286	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
287	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
288	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
289	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
290	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
291	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
292	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
293	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
294	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
295	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
296	
297	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
298	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the flat-scoped
299	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
300	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
301	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
302	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
303	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
304	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
305	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
306	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
307	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
308	\end{remark}

310	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
311	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
312	
313	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
314	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
315	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
316	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
317	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
318	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the flat-scoped restriction-compatibility
319	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
320	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
321	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
322	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
323	cover, together with their restriction to the
324	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
325	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
326	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
327	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	[Tensor product commutes with restriction for line bundles]
331	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
333	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
334	  % NOTE (iter-206 review): the prover (task_results/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.lean.md,
335	  % informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md) reports the flat-exactness proof sketch
336	  % below does NOT, as written, formalize: the categorical route lands the goal
337	  % at sheafify((PresheafOfModules.pullback φ.hom).obj (M.val ⊗ N.val)), where
338	  % `PresheafOfModules.pullback` is an ABSTRACT left adjoint with no sectionwise
339	  % formula — so the comparison MAP itself is missing, and flatness only upgrades
340	  % an already-existing map to an iso (it does not construct it). The genuine
341	  % missing ingredient is an oplax-monoidal (base-change) structure on
342	  % `PresheafOfModules.pullback φ`, a multi-file Mathlib gap (sectionwise
343	  % `ModuleCat.extendScalars.Monoidal` exists; the lift through the
344	  % partial-left-adjoint construction does not). The chapter's affine-cover
345	  % flat-exactness proof should be re-derived to construct the comparison map
346	  % (via an affine/gluing route, not the abstract-adjoint route) or the proof
347	  % retargeted to a `mathlib-build` lane; see recommendations.md.
348	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be line
349	  bundles (equivalently: locally-trivial rank-one modules, hence flat), and
350	  let \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be the restriction along an open immersion.
351	  The canonical comparison morphism
352	  \[
353	    (L \otimes_X M)\big|_f
354	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
355	    L\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; M\big|_f
356	  \]
357	  is an isomorphism. The intended Lean target is
358	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
359	\end{lemma}
360	
361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
363	  The statement is local on \(X\), so it suffices to verify it on affine
364	  opens, where both line bundles are presented by \(A\)-modules \(L, M\) over
365	  a commutative ring \(A\) and the comparison is a map of \(A\)-modules whose
366	  formation commutes with \(L \otimes_A (-)\). A line bundle is locally free
367	  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
368	  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
369	  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\)
370	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}, the
371	  \(\mathtt{instProjectiveOfInvertible}\) instance chain). For a flat module
372	  the comparison is then an isomorphism by ordinary exactness, with no
373	  internal-hom machinery: \(\otimes\) is always right-exact, so
374	  \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity, and flatness of \(L\) makes
375	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve injectivity
376	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
377	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
378	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
379	  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
380	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
381	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}): for invertible \(L\), the map
382	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) is bijective iff \(g\) is. Applying
383	  this to the (bijective) structural comparison map for the second factor
384	  \(M\) under restriction yields bijectivity of the full comparison, i.e.\
385	  the asserted isomorphism. The affine isomorphisms are compatible along an
386	  affine cover, so they glue to the global isomorphism.
387	
388	  This is the elementary flat-exactness replacement for the
389	  monoidal-closed / \(\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\,W\) route recorded in
390	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}: that route was forced only
391	  because the analogous \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability fails for a
392	  \emph{non-flat} module \(F\). Restricting the scope to line bundles makes
393	  the flatness hypothesis automatic, so the comparison is an isomorphism by
394	  module-theoretic exactness alone.
395	\end{proof}
396	
397	\begin{lemma}
398	
399	\leanok
400	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
401	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
402	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
403	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
404	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
405	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
406	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
407	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
408	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
409	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
410	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
411	\end{lemma}
412	
413	\begin{proof}
414	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
415	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
416	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
417	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
418	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
419	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
420	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
421	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
422	\end{proof}
423	
424	\begin{lemma}
425	[Associativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
426	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
427	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
428	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
429	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
430	  bundles. Then there exists an isomorphism
431	  \[
432	    (L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N
433	      \;\cong\;
434	    L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)
435	  \]
436	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently the proposition
437	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N \cong
438	  L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)\bigr)\) holds.
439	\end{lemma}
440	
441	\begin{proof}
442	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
443	  Choose a common affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\)
444	  trivialising \(L, M, N\) simultaneously. By
445	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} every tensor product
446	  occurring on either side is again a line bundle, hence flat, so
447	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} applies and both sides restrict to the
448	  iterated tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules on \(U_i\). There the associator
449	  \((L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i) \otimes_{A_i} N_i \cong
450	  L_i \otimes_{A_i} (M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i)\) is the standard associativity
451	  isomorphism of the tensor product of modules over a commutative ring. These
452	  local isomorphisms agree on overlaps --- naturality of the module
453	  associator against the restriction-comparison isomorphisms of
454	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and therefore glue to a global
455	  isomorphism. Only the \emph{existence} of the global isomorphism is
456	  asserted; no compatibility with a pentagon identity is claimed or needed.
457	\end{proof}
458	
459	\begin{lemma}
460	[Unit law for $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
461	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
462	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
463	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) a line
464	  bundle. Then \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\) and
465	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong L\); equivalently
466	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L)\) holds (and
467	  symmetrically on the right).
468	\end{lemma}
469	
470	\begin{proof}
471	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
472	  On a trivialising affine cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(L\), restriction
473	  commutes with \(\otimes_X\) by \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and the
474	  structure sheaf restricts to \(A_i\) as an \(A_i\)-module; the unitor
475	  \(A_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i \cong L_i\) is the standard left-unit isomorphism of
476	  the tensor product. These local isomorphisms glue to a global isomorphism
477	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\); the right-unit case is identical.
478	\end{proof}
479	
480	\begin{lemma}
481	[Commutativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
482	  \label{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
483	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
484	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
485	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
486	  bundles. Then \(L \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X L\); equivalently
487	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(L \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X L)\) holds.
488	\end{lemma}
489	
490	\begin{proof}
491	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
492	  On a common trivialising affine cover, restriction commutes with
493	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}); locally the braiding
494	  \(L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i \cong M_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i\) is the standard
495	  symmetry isomorphism of the tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules. The local
496	  symmetries glue to a global isomorphism.
497	\end{proof}
498	
499	\begin{lemma}
500	
501	\leanok
502	[Inverse of an invertible module]
503	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
504	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
505	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
506	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
507	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
508	  bundle. The dual
509	  \[
510	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
511	  \]
512	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
513	  isomorphism
514	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
515	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
516	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
517	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
518	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
519	\end{lemma}
520	
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
523	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
524	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
525	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
526	  The contraction map
527	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
528	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
529	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
530	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
531	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
532	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
533	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
534	\end{proof}

537	
538	\leanok
539	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
540	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
541	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
542	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
543	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,

546	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}

566	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
567	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
568	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
569	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}

587	\begin{lemma}
588	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
589	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
590	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
591	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
592	  functor
593	  \[
594	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
595	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
596	  \]
597	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
598	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
599	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
600	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
601	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
602	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
603	  canonical isomorphism
604	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
605	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
606	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
607	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
608	\end{lemma}
609

611	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
612	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
613	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
614	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
615	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
616	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
617	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
618	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the same
619	  flat-exactness / restriction-compatibility argument as in
620	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (extension of scalars along \(A \to B\)
621	  is itself flat, so the comparison is an isomorphism). The compatibility
622	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
623	  same cover.
624	\end{proof}

627	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
628	
629	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}

631	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law

633	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
634	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
635	
636	\begin{theorem}
637	
638	\leanok
639	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
640	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}

650	
651	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),

672	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
673	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
674	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
675	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a

677	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
678	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
679	  \[
680	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
681	  \]
682	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
683	  abelian-group structure with addition
684	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
685	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
686	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
687	  inverse
688	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
689	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
690	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
691	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
692	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
693	  map
694	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
695	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
696	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
697	\end{theorem}
698	
699	\begin{proof}
700	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
701	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
702	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
703	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
704	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
705	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
706	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
707	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
708	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
709	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
710	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
711	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
712	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
713	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
714	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
715	  symmetrically in the second argument. The group axioms are then
716	  \emph{exactly} the four existence-of-isomorphism facts established above,
717	  read on isomorphism classes:
718	  \begin{itemize}
719	    \item \textbf{associativity} \(([L]+[M])+[N] = [L]+([M]+[N])\) is
720	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso};
721	    \item \textbf{neutral element} \([\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}] + [L] = [L]\)
722	      (and on the right) is \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso};
723	    \item \textbf{commutativity} \([L]+[M]=[M]+[L]\) is
724	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso};
725	    \item \textbf{inverse} \([L] + [L^{-1}] = [\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T}]\),
726	      with \(L^{-1}=\mathcal{H}om(L,\mathcal{O}_{C\times_k T})\), is
727	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
728	  \end{itemize}
729	  Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the existence of an isomorphism ---
730	  so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon, triangle, hexagon) is invoked or
731	  required; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is assembled
732	  directly from these four \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\)
733	  statements. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group:
734	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\)
735	  (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup})
736	  transported from
737	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\), the
738	  iso-class arithmetic being the family of existence-of-iso facts
739	  \([M \otimes N] = [M]\cdot[N]\) and \([M^{\vee}] = [M]^{-1}\); here the same
740	  shape is transported to the scheme-level line-bundle subcategory
741	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). (The scheme-level Picard
742	  group is genuinely absent from Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}
743	  records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project
744	  supplies it lightly.) The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's
745	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), mirroring Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)
746	  verbatim.
747	
748	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
749	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
750	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
751	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
752	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
753	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
754	
755	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
756	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
757	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
758	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
759	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
760	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
761	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
762	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
763	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
764	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
765	\end{proof}

  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure

\end{proof}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of (commutative) structure-sheaf-valued
  presheaves of rings induced by the open immersion \(f\), so that
  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor
  on presheaves of modules. The proof proceeds by first \emph{constructing}
  the comparison morphism as the canonical base-change map of a left adjoint,
  and only then upgrading it to an isomorphism using flatness. The earlier
  framing --- that flatness alone produces the isomorphism --- is incorrect:
  the abstract pullback has no sectionwise formula, so there is no map to
  which flatness could be applied until the map has been built.

  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The
  restriction \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion
  \(f\) is naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom}).\mathrm{obj}(-)\),
  via the present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\).
  It therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism
  for the abstract pullback functor.

  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
   \;\cong\;
   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi.\mathrm{hom} \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence
  the whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the
  base-change comparison
  \[
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
      \;\longrightarrow\;
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
      \;\otimes\;
    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
  \]
  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\). (This corrects the older
  claim that sheafification does not commute with pullback: it does, and the
  required natural isomorphism is available.)

  \emph{Step 3 (the comparison map is the mate of pushforward).} The functor
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is a left adjoint --- it is
  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\)
  through the present adjunction
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\,\varphi
    : \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
  The base-change comparison of Step 2 is exactly the oplax-monoidal
  comparison \(\delta\) of this left adjoint, obtained by the mate
  construction \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Adjunction.leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Functor}), whose data are
  \(\delta_{A,B} = (\mathrm{adj}.\mathrm{homEquiv})^{-1}\bigl(
    (\eta_A \otimes \eta_B) \circ \mu_{\cdot}\bigr)\) for the unit \(\eta\) of
  the adjunction and the lax structure \(\mu\) of the right adjoint. The sole
  hypothesis of that construction is that the right adjoint be lax monoidal,
  i.e.\ \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\); this is
  furnished by \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}. The comparison map is
  thus produced \emph{abstractly}, with no sectionwise formula required.

  \emph{Step 4 (flatness upgrades \(\delta\) to an isomorphism).} It remains
  to check that, for line bundles, this already-constructed \(\delta\) is an
  isomorphism --- and \emph{this} is where flatness enters. The check is
  local, so it suffices to verify it sectionwise, where the comparison is the
  ordinary base-change map of modules along a ring map and \(\delta\)
  specialises to \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,(-)\) applied to the
  structural comparison for the second factor. A line bundle is locally free
  of rank one, hence finitely generated projective, hence \emph{flat}: in
  Mathlib's vocabulary \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,A\,L\) supplies
  \(\mathtt{Module.Projective}\) and therefore \(\mathtt{Module.Flat}\,A\,L\).
  Tensor is always right-exact, so \(L \otimes_A (-)\) preserves surjectivity,
  and flatness of \(L\) makes \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\) preserve
  injectivity (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\),
  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}); the two together force
  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,L\,g\) to be bijective whenever \(g\) is.
  Mathlib packages precisely this for invertible modules as
  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible.lTensor\_bijective\_iff}\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}). We stress that this step
  \emph{upgrades} the comparison \(\delta\) of Step 3 to an isomorphism; it
  does \emph{not} construct the map, and is not a substitute for Step 3.

  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise/affine isomorphisms of Step 4 are compatible
  with the restriction maps, so by \(\mathtt{hom\_ext}\) they assemble into a
  single isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules; transporting back through
  the isomorphisms of Steps 1--2 yields the asserted global isomorphism
  \((L \otimes_X M)|_f \cong L|_f \otimes_U M|_f\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
  \[
    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
      \;\;:=\;\;
    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
  \]
  is lax monoidal. This supplies the hypothesis
  \([(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi).\mathtt{LaxMonoidal}]\) required by the
  mate construction \(\mathtt{leftAdjointOplaxMonoidal}\) in the proof of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and is the \emph{sole} genuinely
  project-side ingredient that construction depends on.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).

  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
\end{proof}
